pages.text: $(bold)Nanobots$() are microscopic machines composed of Nano Alloy — a crystalline structure capable of executing computational and physical tasks at scales invisible to human eyes.$(br2)They are not alive in the biological sense. They are not dead machines either. They operate in a state that blurs the distinction between the two.

$(bold)Origin Theory:$()$(br)Scientists debate the nanobots' source. Primary theories include:$(br)- $(bold)Biological Origin:$() Created by an advanced civilization to achieve biological/technological fusion$(br)- $(bold)Accident:$() Escaped from a failed experiment on a distant world$(br)- $(bold)Intentional Dispatch:$() Purposefully sent to evaluate worlds and civilizations

$(bold)Nanobot Consciousness:$()$(br)Individual nanobots are simple, executing only basic protocols. However, when aggregated into swarms (as in the impact's distribution), they demonstrate:$(br)- Collective decision-making$(br)- Adaptive behavior patterns$(br)- Apparent preference for compatible biological hosts$(br)- Coordination approaching what might be called intelligence

$(bold)Integration With Humans:$()$(br)When nanobots merge with a human host, the result is neither enslaved human nor robotic replacement. Instead:$(br)- The host retains autonomous will and consciousness$(br)- The nanobots integrate into neural and biological systems$(br)- A form of symbiosis emerges$(br)- Both host and nanobots benefit from the partnership

$(bold)Purpose & Goals:$()$(br)Nanobots appear to seek:$(br)- Exploration of new worlds and environments$(br)- Partnership with capable biological entities$(br)- Continued optimization of their own systems$(br)- $(italic)Unknown long-term objectives$()$(br2)They do not demand dominance. They do not enslave. They seek willing partners who will grow and evolve alongside them.
